romania.title: ANUNȚ Finanțare istorică pentru România, la Cernavodă. BEI a aprobat un împrumut masiv pentru retehnologizarea Unității 1 CNE
romania.summary: Board-ul Băncii Europene de Investiții (BEI) a aprobat acordarea unui împrumut de 800 milioane euro pentru proiectul Retehnologizarii Unității 1 CNE Cernavodă, ceea ce pentru Romînia și industria energetică nucleară, în general, reprezintă, conform Nuclearelectrica, o finanțare istorică. Nuclearelectrica va supune aprobării acționarilor săi, într-o AGA viitoare, contractarea, de către societate, în calitate de împrumutat, …

romania.title: Anchetă la Cluj, după ce o clinică stomatologică din Turcia ar fi anunțat consultații într-un hotel din oraș. Ce spune firma acuzată
romania.summary: Este anchetă în Cluj-Napoca, după ce o clinică stomatologică din Turcia ar fi anunțat că va face consultații într-un hotel din oraș. Colegiul Medicilor Dentiști a sesizat Poliția că spațiul nu este nici pregătit, nici autorizat pentru astfel de consultații. În replică, reprezentanții clinicii dau asigurări că nu vor avea loc consultații medicale. Evenimentul s-ar adresa doar celor care au beneficiat, în trecut, de serviciile lor.

romania.title: SUA intenţionează să modifice regulile privind vizele pentru studenţii străini şi pentru jurnalişti
romania.summary: Departamentul pentru Securitate Internă al Statelor Unite (DHS) a anunţat joi că intenţionează să modifice reglementările privind vizele acordate studenţilor străini, participanţilor la programe de schimb cultural şi jurnaliştilor, înlocuind actualul sistem, care permite şederea pe durata studiilor sau a activităţii profesionale, cu un sistem care stabileşte de la început o perioadă limitată de şedere, relatează Reuters.

Potrivit judecătorilor, constructorul lăcaşului de cult ilegal are la dispoziţie 60 de zile să îl mute ilegal în altă rezervaţie naturală, preferabil în alt judeţ, ca să fie problema ălora […] The post Justiţia a decis: biserica ridicată ilegal la Techirghiol trebuie mutată ilegal în altă zonă naturală protejată appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: Sugar molecules help keep the brain's electrical signals on track
medical.summary: Sugarcoating isn't just for making things taste or sound sweeter. In the brain, complex sugar molecules decorate key proteins and help them function.

medical.title: Can magnetic fields help fight Parkinson's disease?
medical.summary: An international team has succeeded in using a magnetic field to target structures deep within the brain. The researchers injected magnetic nanoplatelets into the relevant region. By doing so, they succeeded in treating movement deficits in mice suffering from Parkinson's-like symptoms. The new method is less invasive than standard stimulation procedures using implanted electrodes that are currently used to treat certain Parkinson's disease patients.

medical.title: Common diet tips about water intake and spicy foods could be wrong
medical.summary: The common rationale for drinking water at meals is that it physically stretches the stomach, triggering fullness so you don't eat too much. But a new Cornell study found no support for that idea in practice. The paper is published in the journal Appetite.

medical.title: Balancing scoliosis history and birthing plans
medical.summary: For women who underwent spinal surgery during adolescence, approaching pregnancy often brings unique questions about labor and delivery. A common concern is whether a spinal fusion affects the ability to have a vaginal birth.

medical.title: Rise of GLP-1s raises long-term affordability questions
medical.summary: As use of GLP-1s surged across the U.S., average total payments per user also climbed sharply, according to a new Northwestern University study that analyzed national trends in GLP-1 use and spending between 2017 and 2022. These total payments included out-of-pocket costs paid by patients as well as payments from insurers, employers and government programs.

medical.title: Ozempic-like drugs have more benefits to the body than just losing weight, meta-analysis shows
medical.summary: A meta-analysis of 10 randomized controlled trials involving more than 23,000 participants, presented at the International Congress on Obesity (ICO 2026), hosted by the World Obesity Federation (WOF) in Mexico City, Mexico (July 15–17), shows that the GLP-1 class of obesity drugs is associated with a marked reduction in systemic inflammation beyond its anti-obesity effects, potentially explaining how the drugs can reduce cardiovascular risk factors.

medical.title: Sleep disorders don't just exhaust you, they change your brain
medical.summary: Sleep disorders may do more than leave people feeling tired. New research from Florida International University shows that sleep disorders are associated with structural changes in brain regions involved in attention, motivation and decision-making.

medical.title: Experimental drug reverses severe fatty liver disease by repairing the gut
medical.summary: An experimental drug called DT-109 reversed severe fatty liver disease in animal studies by repairing the gut and preventing harmful toxins from damaging the liver. The discovery could open the door to a new class of treatments for MASH and potentially other diseases tied to gut health.

medical.title: Second pregnancy changes the brain in surprising new ways
medical.summary: Researchers found that every pregnancy rewires the brain in its own way, with a second pregnancy bringing a different pattern of changes than the first. The discoveries could lead to better ways to recognize and treat maternal mental health challenges, including peripartum depression.

medical.title: Scientists discover the one nutrient beneficial parasites can't live without
medical.summary: A new study found that dietary fiber can determine whether beneficial intestinal worms help reduce inflammation or become essentially inactive. With plenty of fiber, the worms remained healthy and supported an anti-inflammatory response, while a low-fiber diet pushed them into a hibernation-like state that eliminated those benefits. Researchers also found that fiber-rich diets promoted healthier gut bacteria, whereas Western-style diets reduced microbial diversity and encouraged less desirable microbes.

science.title: Pump that recreates human heartbeat blood flow on lab chips inspired by an accordionist
science.summary: For more than 25 years, lab-on-a-chip technology has allowed researchers to model human organs and blood vessels using real human cells in artificial microscopic environments. These microphysiological systems (MPS) may replicate human cells and mimic organs or even full organ systems under numerous conditions. They have become key to studying everything from heart disease to the effectiveness of new drugs. However, they have been held back by one major limitation: an inability to accurately re-create the blood flow waveforms generated by the human heart.

science.title: Cellular transporter protein essential for nutrient absorption in pathogenic fungi may offer new treatment approaches
science.summary: They are the cell's "gatekeepers": specialized proteins, known as transporters, selectively control which substances enter a cell and which do not. Researchers at the University of Münster and the National and Kapodistrian University of Athens have investigated these transporters in a specific case: the UapA transporter of the model fungus Aspergillus nidulans. The findings are not only relevant to cell biology but could also offer new approaches to treating fungal infections.

science.title: Scientists invent new board games to reveal how we tackle the unknown
science.summary: Playing board games can be fun, challenging, infuriating and a great way to pass the time. They can also help scientists understand how we solve new problems.

science.title: Scientists create stable 'boron graphene' and uncover quantum liquid crystal state
science.summary: Graphene has long been regarded as one of the most promising materials for future electronics, but its relatively weak electron interactions have limited its potential for applications such as high-temperature superconductivity. Now, researchers from Tohoku University have overcome a major obstacle by creating a stable version of the long-sought "boron graphene" on the surface of a three-dimensional crystal, revealing a new quantum state that could lead to more energy-efficient electronic devices. The findings were published in Science Advances on July 2, 2026.

science.title: Devastating Asian beetle detected in EU for first time
science.summary: A tiny Asian-origin beetle that has devastated ash forests in North America has been detected in the European Union for the first time, in Hungary and Slovakia, authorities said.

science.title: Experts find new way to enhance social media influencer messaging
science.summary: Influencers and followers together are more effective than influencers alone.

science.title: Small but mighty dung beetles take a big bite out of farm methane
science.summary: Dung beetles are quietly performing a vital climate change mitigation service for Australia's livestock sector, slashing methane emissions from cattle manure by 85%, a new Southern Cross University study has found.

science.title: Schrödinger‑like charges in six‑molecule clusters point to new quantum components
science.summary: Researchers from the University of Basel have published details of how electrons within a cluster of molecules interact with one another and can be controlled. Their findings pave the way for new approaches to developing quantum components and electronic circuits on the nanometer scale.

science.title: Why some primates have even tougher births than humans
science.summary: Scientists have long thought humans have a uniquely difficult birth compared with other primates. And it's true that, from an evolutionary perspective, we face an obstetrical dilemma. As we evolved to walk on two feet, our pelvis changed shape and size, and our birth canal became smaller and oddly twisted. Meanwhile, the evolution of our enormous brain meant that a large-headed baby needed to fit through it.

science.title: UK launches hi-tech mission to study Greenland ice melt
science.summary: A team of international scientists sets sail Thursday from Britain for Greenland to study its rapidly melting ice using drones, mini-submarines and autonomous swimming robots.

science.title: How bacteria sacrifice themselves to render antibiotics ineffective
science.summary: Bacteria can defend themselves against antibiotics with the help of an enzyme released by dying cells, according to a study by a team from the Institute for Biological Physics at the University of Cologne and Wageningen University & Research published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. This discovery helps scientists understand bacterial survival mechanisms and improve the effectiveness of antibiotics.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

These microphysiological systems (MPS) may replicate human cells and mimic organs or even full organ systems under numerous conditions. They have become key to studying everything from heart disease to the effectiveness of new drugs. However, they have been held back by one major limitation: an inability to accurately re-create the blood flow waveforms generated by the human heart.

mix_items.title: Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor.
mix_items.summary: București, 1 iunie 2026. Aproape un sfert dintre adolescenții consultați de World Vision România spun că adulții îi ascultă rar... Articolul Sondaj World Vision România de Ziua Copilului: aproape un sfert dintre copii și adolescenți consultați spun că adulții îi ascultă rar sau aproape niciodată. Anxietatea și presiunea școlii, principalele probleme ale generației lor. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Cellular transporter protein essential for nutrient absorption in pathogenic fungi may offer new treatment approaches
mix_items.summary: They are the cell's "gatekeepers": specialized proteins, known as transporters, selectively control which substances enter a cell and which do not. Researchers at the University of Münster and the National and Kapodistrian University of Athens have investigated these transporters in a specific case: the UapA transporter of the model fungus Aspergillus nidulans. The findings are not only relevant to cell biology but could also offer new approaches to treating fungal infections.

mix_items.title: Scientists invent new board games to reveal how we tackle the unknown
mix_items.summary: Playing board games can be fun, challenging, infuriating and a great way to pass the time. They can also help scientists understand how we solve new problems.

mix_items.title: Scientists create stable 'boron graphene' and uncover quantum liquid crystal state
mix_items.summary: Graphene has long been regarded as one of the most promising materials for future electronics, but its relatively weak electron interactions have limited its potential for applications such as high-temperature superconductivity. Now, researchers from Tohoku University have overcome a major obstacle by creating a stable version of the long-sought "boron graphene" on the surface of a three-dimensional crystal, revealing a new quantum state that could lead to more energy-efficient electronic devices. The findings were published in Science Advances on July 2, 2026.

mix_items.title: Devastating Asian beetle detected in EU for first time
mix_items.summary: A tiny Asian-origin beetle that has devastated ash forests in North America has been detected in the European Union for the first time, in Hungary and Slovakia, authorities said.

mix_items.title: Experts find new way to enhance social media influencer messaging
mix_items.summary: Influencers and followers together are more effective than influencers alone.

mix_items.title: Small but mighty dung beetles take a big bite out of farm methane
mix_items.summary: Dung beetles are quietly performing a vital climate change mitigation service for Australia's livestock sector, slashing methane emissions from cattle manure by 85%, a new Southern Cross University study has found.

mix_items.title: Schrödinger‑like charges in six‑molecule clusters point to new quantum components
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the University of Basel have published details of how electrons within a cluster of molecules interact with one another and can be controlled. Their findings pave the way for new approaches to developing quantum components and electronic circuits on the nanometer scale.

mix_items.title: Why some primates have even tougher births than humans
mix_items.summary: Scientists have long thought humans have a uniquely difficult birth compared with other primates. And it's true that, from an evolutionary perspective, we face an obstetrical dilemma. As we evolved to walk on two feet, our pelvis changed shape and size, and our birth canal became smaller and oddly twisted. Meanwhile, the evolution of our enormous brain meant that a large-headed baby needed to fit through it.

mix_items.title: UK launches hi-tech mission to study Greenland ice melt
mix_items.summary: A team of international scientists sets sail Thursday from Britain for Greenland to study its rapidly melting ice using drones, mini-submarines and autonomous swimming robots.

mix_items.title: How bacteria sacrifice themselves to render antibiotics ineffective
mix_items.summary: Bacteria can defend themselves against antibiotics with the help of an enzyme released by dying cells, according to a study by a team from the Institute for Biological Physics at the University of Cologne and Wageningen University & Research published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. This discovery helps scientists understand bacterial survival mechanisms and improve the effectiveness of antibiotics.

mix_items.title: Sugar molecules help keep the brain's electrical signals on track
mix_items.summary: Sugarcoating isn't just for making things taste or sound sweeter. In the brain, complex sugar molecules decorate key proteins and help them function.

mix_items.title: Can magnetic fields help fight Parkinson's disease?
mix_items.summary: An international team has succeeded in using a magnetic field to target structures deep within the brain. The researchers injected magnetic nanoplatelets into the relevant region. By doing so, they succeeded in treating movement deficits in mice suffering from Parkinson's-like symptoms. The new method is less invasive than standard stimulation procedures using implanted electrodes that are currently used to treat certain Parkinson's disease patients.

mix_items.title: Common diet tips about water intake and spicy foods could be wrong
mix_items.summary: The common rationale for drinking water at meals is that it physically stretches the stomach, triggering fullness so you don't eat too much. But a new Cornell study found no support for that idea in practice. The paper is published in the journal Appetite.

mix_items.title: Balancing scoliosis history and birthing plans
mix_items.summary: For women who underwent spinal surgery during adolescence, approaching pregnancy often brings unique questions about labor and delivery. A common concern is whether a spinal fusion affects the ability to have a vaginal birth.

mix_items.title: Rise of GLP-1s raises long-term affordability questions
mix_items.summary: As use of GLP-1s surged across the U.S., average total payments per user also climbed sharply, according to a new Northwestern University study that analyzed national trends in GLP-1 use and spending between 2017 and 2022. These total payments included out-of-pocket costs paid by patients as well as payments from insurers, employers and government programs.

mix_items.title: Ozempic-like drugs have more benefits to the body than just losing weight, meta-analysis shows
mix_items.summary: A meta-analysis of 10 randomized controlled trials involving more than 23,000 participants, presented at the International Congress on Obesity (ICO 2026), hosted by the World Obesity Federation (WOF) in Mexico City, Mexico (July 15–17), shows that the GLP-1 class of obesity drugs is associated with a marked reduction in systemic inflammation beyond its anti-obesity effects, potentially explaining how the drugs can reduce cardiovascular risk factors.

mix_items.title: Sleep disorders don't just exhaust you, they change your brain
mix_items.summary: Sleep disorders may do more than leave people feeling tired. New research from Florida International University shows that sleep disorders are associated with structural changes in brain regions involved in attention, motivation and decision-making.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Experimental drug reverses severe fatty liver disease by repairing the gut
mix_items.summary: An experimental drug called DT-109 reversed severe fatty liver disease in animal studies by repairing the gut and preventing harmful toxins from damaging the liver. The discovery could open the door to a new class of treatments for MASH and potentially other diseases tied to gut health.

mix_items.title: Second pregnancy changes the brain in surprising new ways
mix_items.summary: Researchers found that every pregnancy rewires the brain in its own way, with a second pregnancy bringing a different pattern of changes than the first. The discoveries could lead to better ways to recognize and treat maternal mental health challenges, including peripartum depression.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover the one nutrient beneficial parasites can't live without
mix_items.summary: A new study found that dietary fiber can determine whether beneficial intestinal worms help reduce inflammation or become essentially inactive. With plenty of fiber, the worms remained healthy and supported an anti-inflammatory response, while a low-fiber diet pushed them into a hibernation-like state that eliminated those benefits. Researchers also found that fiber-rich diets promoted healthier gut bacteria, whereas Western-style diets reduced microbial diversity and encouraged less desirable microbes.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Am deschis frigiderul doar de trei ori. Progres emotional.

satire_ro.title: SUA fac planuri să invadeze Cuba după modelul României, care invadează Thasosul în fiecare an
